misconceptionSummary: Students think factors are just the small numbers that divide in, not recognising that every number has the number itself as a factor.

visuals.ruleText: A factor of n divides into n exactly — no remainder
visuals.subText: Every number is a factor of itself. 1 is a factor of every number.

steps.narration: A factor is a number that divides into the target exactly. Every whole number is a factor of itself — because any number divided by itself is one, with no remainder. And one is a factor of everything, because everything divided by one equals itself.

Think of factors as rectangle dimensions. If you can tile a rectangle with exactly twenty-four unit squares using whole-number side lengths, those lengths are a factor pair. Three by eight gives twenty-four — so both three and eight are factors.

lines.text: Each gives a pair of factors
No other whole-number rectangle fits 24 tiles

steps.narration: Every factor pair corresponds to a distinct rectangle. One by twenty-four, two by twelve, three by eight, four by six — these are all the whole-number rectangles with area twenty-four. Each edge length is a factor.

visuals.label: Factors = whole-number rectangle dimensions with area n

steps.narration: One, two, three, four, six, eight, twelve, twenty-four. Each pair of factors is a pair of rectangle dimensions — a geometric way to see why factors always come in pairs.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: not including the number itself as a factor. Every number n is a factor of itself because n ÷ n = 1 exactly. Both 1 and n are always factors of n.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: One and the number itself are always factors — every number divides into itself exactly once. Factors are the whole-number rectangle dimensions that give the number as area.
